models_compared.name: KenPom (Ken Pomeroy)
models_compared.methodology: Adjusted offensive and defensive efficiency per 100 possessions, adjusted for opponent quality. Uses tempo-free statistics to compare teams regardless of pace.

accuracy.championship_profile: All but 2 champions since 2002 were top-20 in both AdjO and AdjD

weaknesses: Drops to near coin-flip on close games
Doesn't account for roster changes mid-season well

models_compared.name: FiveThirtyEight (Nate Silver / ABC News)
models_compared.methodology: Blends 6 computer power ratings (KenPom, Sagarin, Sonny Moore, LRMC, ESPN BPI, own Elo) at 75% weight + human rankings (AP poll, coaches poll, NCAA S-curve) at 25% weight. Win probability formula: P = 1/(1+10^(-travel_adjusted_diff * 30.464/400)). Adjusts for travel distance, injuries (via Win Shares), preseason rankings.

accuracy.notes: Refined since 2011 launch, adopted live-updating in 2016. Multiple validation studies referenced but specific Brier scores not published.

strengths: Multi-source blend smooths out individual model errors
Travel distance adjustment is unique and valuable
Injury adjustment uses Win Shares methodology
Season initialization reverts Elo to conference mean
Accounts for fewer upsets in tournament vs regular season
weaknesses: No longer actively maintained after FiveThirtyEight dissolution
Human ranking component may introduce bias
Doesn't account for in-game personnel changes

models_compared.name: ESPN BPI (Basketball Power Index)
models_compared.methodology: Proprietary model using game-by-game results, schedule strength, and opponent quality. One of six systems used in FiveThirtyEight blend.

accuracy.notes: Used in ESPN bubble watch and bracketology projections

models_compared.name: Bart Torvik (T-Rank)
models_compared.methodology: Similar to KenPom but adds experience/returning production metrics. Used in BartTorvik bracketology consensus.

accuracy.notes: Commonly used alongside KenPom in consensus models

models_compared.name: Sagarin Ratings
models_compared.methodology: Rating system that produces predicted point spreads between any two teams. Agreed with picking highest seeds on 152/188 games.

models_compared.name: TeamRankings
models_compared.methodology: Combines multiple rating systems. Offers pool optimization via PoolGenius bracket builder. Subscribers win 3.1x more often than expected.

accuracy.pool_optimization: 53% of subscribers won at least one prize across recent tournaments

models_compared.name: LRMC (Logistic Regression Markov Chain)
models_compared.methodology: Joel Sokol's model using logistic regression combined with Markov chain techniques to rate teams. Part of FiveThirtyEight blend.

accuracy.notes: Academic model with solid theoretical foundation

models_compared.name: EvanMiya CBB Analytics
models_compared.methodology: Modern analytics platform with team, player, and lineup metrics. Focus on player-level impact data.

best_features.details: Teams with top-10 AEM have won 28 of 39 national championships. The single most powerful predictor of tournament success.
75% of national champions since 2000 ranked top-20 nationally. 35 of 40 Final Four teams in top-25 defensive efficiency. Defense doesn't go cold the way shooting does.
best_features.how_to_use: Points allowed per 100 possessions, adjusted for opponent offensive quality

best_features.details: Strong offense is the most predictive indicator for team success overall. But 9 of 10 tournament teams with top-10 offense but outside top-100 defense lost in Round 1.
best_features.how_to_use: Points scored per 100 possessions, adjusted for opponent defensive quality

best_features.details: Most influential feature in ML models (65.9% feature importance in one study). Previous season win record also significant.
best_features.how_to_use: Total wins, conference wins, win% against tournament-caliber opponents

best_features.details: Normalizes records by opponent quality. A 25-5 record in Big 12 is not same as 25-5 in Patriot League.
best_features.how_to_use: Simple Rating System (SRS) or KenPom SOS metric

best_features.details: Big X-factor in upsets. Understanding style-of-play clashes is important. Controlled tempo reduces game variance. Key variable in 10-variable predictive model.
best_features.how_to_use: Possessions per 40 minutes, adjusted for opponent

best_features.details: Consistent 3PT shooting (above 36%) is an upset predictor. Hot shooting beyond the arc has fueled notable upsets. But high variance makes it risky to rely on.

best_features.feature: Free Throw Rate / Free Throw Shooting
best_features.details: Critical in close tournament games. Solid free throw shooting correlates with late-game success.
best_features.how_to_use: FT%, FT rate (FTA/FGA), opponent FT rate

best_features.details: Included in key predictive models. Both offensive turnover rate and defensive steal/forced turnover rate matter.
best_features.how_to_use: Turnovers per 100 possessions on both ends

best_features.details: Defensive rebounds identified as noteworthy feature in ML analysis. Offensive rebounding percentage gives second-chance points.
3+ experienced rotation players is an upset predictor trait. College basketball stars can account for 30%+ of team offensive production.
best_features.how_to_use: Years of experience in rotation, minutes by upperclassmen

best_features.details: Historical performance in high-stress tournament situations matters. Coaching tactical decisions are upset factor.
best_features.how_to_use: Tournament wins, Final Four appearances, years of tournament coaching experience

best_features.details: Power conference membership matters. Mid-major underseeding is systematic — these teams may be undervalued.
best_features.how_to_use: Power 6 conference flag, conference KenPom average

best_features.details: One model weights at 30% of projection. Captures momentum and injury impacts. But conference tournament performance may be noise.
best_features.how_to_use: Win%, efficiency metrics over last 10 games. NOTE: Our CLAUDE.md explicitly excludes this — may want to reconsider.

best_features.details: FiveThirtyEight found travel distance effects tantamount to home/road advantage despite neutral court. Coast-to-coast vs nearby matters.
best_features.how_to_use: Distance from team campus to game site

kaggle_insights.details: Gradient Boosted Trees achieved 76.37% accuracy across 100 train/test splits. Neural networks: 67%, SVM: 65%, KNN: 63%, Logistic Regression: 63%, Random Forest: 61%. However, logistic regression shows most consistent results with lowest standard deviation.

Despite being simpler, logistic regression frequently outperforms complex models. This is likely because: (1) limited training data favors simpler models, (2) tournament outcomes have inherent randomness that complex models overfit to, (3) neural networks essentially learn logistic regression internally.

kaggle_insights.finding: LSTM with Brier Loss is State-of-Art
kaggle_insights.details: LSTM model trained with Brier loss achieved lowest Brier score of 0.1589 for NCAA basketball outcomes. Transformer architecture achieved higher discriminative power but worse calibration.

kaggle_insights.finding: XGBoost Can Achieve 90% Training Accuracy But Overfits
kaggle_insights.details: XGBoost models perform very well in training but tend to perform middle-of-pack when generating actual tournament brackets. Overfitting to training data is the main risk.
Team representation as 16-dimensional vector with matchup encoded as difference between vectors outperforms raw stats. Efficiency Ratio (AdjO/AdjD) and Upset Propensity metrics improve predictions.
Best performing model used 115,113 games from 1993-2016. Larger historical datasets help, sourced from Kaggle and Sports-Reference.

kaggle_insights.finding: Simple Beats Complex for Small Datasets
kaggle_insights.details: In some analyses, simple linear regression outperformed all other ML algorithms. With limited tournament games per year (63-67), complex models lack sufficient data to generalize.
One winning team added Twitter sentiment analysis data to measure game excitement, combined with leaderboard switches and suspense metrics.

vegas_vs_models.key_finding: Vegas and computer models perform comparably — neither dramatically outperforms the other or simple seed-based predictions

vegas_accuracy.seed_agreement: Vegas agreed with picking highest seeds on 156/188 games (83%)
vegas_accuracy.notes: Vegas relies heavily on seeds but adds market-derived adjustments

model_accuracy.sagarin_seed_agreement: Sagarin agreed with highest seed on 152/188 games (80.9%)
model_accuracy.ml_vs_seed_baseline: There is not a great increase in ML accuracy against simply choosing highest seed
model_accuracy.kenpom_tournament: 70.4% winner prediction overall, drops to ~53% on close games

when_models_beat_vegas: When models identify systematic mid-major underseeding
When models capture recent injuries before lines adjust
When models use tempo/style matchup analysis Vegas ignores
Early-season odds before market has full information
when_vegas_beats_models: Vegas has more information sources (insider info, injury reports, money flows)
Close games where statistical edge is minimal
High-profile matchups where public attention drives accuracy

de_vigging_methodology.problem: Sportsbook moneyline odds don't sum to 100% — the excess is the vig (juice). Example: -180/+155 sums to 103.5%, meaning 3.5% is vig.
de_vigging_methodology.solution: Remove vig by proportionally reducing both sides so implied probabilities sum to 100%.
de_vigging_methodology.formula: For each side: true_prob = implied_prob / sum_of_implied_probs
de_vigging_methodology.example: Ohio State -180 (implied 64.3%) vs Utah +155 (implied 39.2%). Sum = 103.5%. True probs: OSU = 64.3/103.5 = 62.1%, Utah = 39.2/103.5 = 37.9%
de_vigging_methodology.advanced: Power method and Shin method provide more accurate de-vigging than simple proportional method for heavy favorites

vegas_vs_models.recommendation: Our blend of 40% stats + 45% market + 15% factors is well-supported. Market odds capture collective wisdom efficiently, but stats models add value on matchup-specific insights and systematic biases.

upset_factors.details: The #1 predictor of upsets. Teams with elite defense can contain superior offensive teams through controlled possessions.
Slower pace reduces variance in game outcomes, giving underdogs more chances. Tempo mismatch is a key X-factor in upsets.
Hot shooting beyond the arc has fueled notable tournament upsets. But it cuts both ways — high 3PT variance can doom teams too.
Experience in high-pressure situations matters enormously in single-elimination format.

upset_factors.factor: Guard-Heavy Roster with High Steal Rate
upset_factors.details: Genetic algorithm analysis found lower-seeded teams making deep runs tend to be guard-heavy with high steal rates and high 3PT attempt rates.
Mid-majors are systematically underseeded relative to their efficiency metrics. This creates predictable upset opportunities.
Coaches with deep tournament experience make better tactical adjustments in single-elimination format.
Aggregate statistics miss matchup-specific advantages. A team's style may specifically counter their opponent's approach.

upset_factors.factor: Free Throw Shooting in Close Games
upset_factors.details: Tournament games are disproportionately close. FT shooting in late-game situations separates winners from losers.

5_vs_12.notes: Classic upset spot — 12-seed wins 35.6% of time

1_vs_2.notes: Nearly a coin flip at this stage

championship_by_seed.notes: No team seeded 5, 6, or higher than 8 has reached the title game

bracket_optimization.details: Small pools: play chalk (using KenPom odds, not just seeds). Large pools: must differentiate with contrarian picks. The most common mistake is going overly contrarian in small pools.

bracket_optimization.strategy: Expected Value = Win Probability x Inverse Ownership
bracket_optimization.details: Compare model win probability against public ownership percentage. When a team's win probability exceeds their pick percentage, they offer positive expected value. This is the core of bracket pool optimization.

bracket_optimization.strategy: Be Contrarian in Later Rounds, Not Early Rounds
bracket_optimization.details: Later rounds have higher point values and more leverage. Picking Round 1 upsets for differentiation is low-value (only 10 points). The Final Four and Championship picks are where contrarian value concentrates.
Pick at least one 12-over-5 upset — it has occurred in 32 of the last 39 tournaments. At 35.6% individual probability, NOT picking any 12-seed upset is statistically suboptimal.
97% of 1-seeds have historically reached the Sweet 16. Fade the 8/9 winner against 1-seeds. This is reliable chalk.
Prioritize defense over offense for Final Four selections. 75% of champions ranked top-20 in defensive efficiency.
Conference tournament performance is noise, not signal. Small sample size and fatigue effects make it unreliable.

bracket_optimization.strategy: Avoid Conference Clustering in Final Four
bracket_optimization.details: Don't pick multiple teams from the same conference in the Final Four. Historically unlikely and reduces bracket diversity.
When everyone plays favorites, go for sleeper. When everyone picks upsets, go for favorites. The optimal strategy is the inverse of what the crowd does.
Different scoring systems (1-2-4-8-16-32 vs ESPN standard) change optimal strategy. Higher late-round multipliers increase value of contrarian champion picks.

novel_approaches.details: Generate random population of brackets, evaluate fitness based on expected score, select top performers, crossover and mutate, repeat. Genetic algorithms revealed that lower-seeded teams making deep runs tend to be guard-heavy with high steal rates and high 3PT attempt rates.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: HIGH — aligns with user's Biology Agent concept. Can layer evolutionary optimization on top of stratified importance sampling.

novel_approaches.details: Multi-class classification neural network trained on team profiles, then genetic algorithms used to interpret what makes successful tournament teams. Generated synthetic team profiles that outperformed their seeding.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: HIGH — combines deep learning with evolutionary interpretation

novel_approaches.details: LSTM model trained directly on Brier score loss function achieved best calibration (Brier 0.1589) for NCAA basketball outcomes. Better than training on binary cross-entropy.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: MEDIUM — could inform our probability calibration approach

novel_approaches.details: Transformer model achieved higher discriminative power than LSTM but worse calibration. Suggests attention mechanisms capture game dynamics differently.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: MEDIUM — attention mechanisms parallel the AlphaFold analogy user mentioned

novel_approaches.approach: Monte Carlo Simulation with Dynamic Rating Updates
novel_approaches.details: Simulate tournament thousands of times, but update team ratings BETWEEN rounds based on how teams performed relative to expectations. Captures the reality that Day 1 results affect Day 2 predictions.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: HIGH — addresses user's concern about temporal correlation between rounds

novel_approaches.details: Using CUDA/GPU for massive parallel bracket simulation. Can generate millions of brackets in seconds rather than minutes.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: LOW — we're using NumPy vectorization which should be sufficient for 12M brackets

novel_approaches.details: Kaggle winning team added Twitter sentiment data measuring game excitement. Combined with suspense metrics (last moment winning team took lead) and leaderboard switches.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: LOW-MEDIUM — could inform qualitative adjustments but adds complexity

novel_approaches.details: AI and prediction markets increasingly replacing traditional office pools in 2026. Prediction markets aggregate crowd wisdom with financial incentives for accuracy.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: MEDIUM — could be additional probability source alongside Vegas odds

novel_approaches.details: Instead of one optimal bracket, generate a SET of diversified brackets that collectively maximize chance of winning. Different brackets target different scenarios (chalk, moderate chaos, maximum chaos).
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: HIGH — this IS essentially what our stratified importance sampling does

novel_approaches.details: Instead of a single team rating number, work with a probability distribution (normal distribution) of possible ratings. Sample from the distribution to capture rating uncertainty.
novel_approaches.relevance_to_our_project: MEDIUM — could improve our simulation by adding rating uncertainty

win_probability_conversion.logistic_regression_approach: Win Prob = 1/(1+e^(-0.163 * predicted_score_differential)). Fitted from historical data.
win_probability_conversion.our_approach: P(A wins) = 1/(1+10^((power_B - power_A)/k)). k calibrated via grid search.
win_probability_conversion.score_differential_std: Standard deviation of score differential is approximately 11.2 points in post-30-second shot clock era.
win_probability_conversion.notes: All three approaches are mathematically equivalent with different parameterizations. Our k-calibration approach is sound.

calibration.notes: Our target of 0.205 is achievable with our blend approach. The LSTM Brier of 0.1589 used deep learning on much larger datasets. Our target is conservative and appropriate.
calibration_tips: Train on Brier loss directly, not binary cross-entropy
Use reliability diagrams to check calibration visually
Combine AUC + accuracy + Brier for complete evaluation

number_of_simulations.typical_approaches: Most public tools use 10,000 to 100,000 simulations
number_of_simulations.our_approach: 12 million brackets (3M per region) with stratified importance sampling
number_of_simulations.notes: Our approach is 120-1200x more simulations than typical. The stratified sampling ensures we cover unlikely but important scenarios.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Adjusted Efficiency Margin is the single most predictive stat
key_takeaways.action: Our 40% weight on AdjEM (KenPom) is well-justified. Consider it could even be higher.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Defense is disproportionately important in March
key_takeaways.action: Our 10% Defensive Efficiency Premium is validated. 75% of champions are top-20 defense.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Logistic regression often beats complex ML models
key_takeaways.action: Our logistic function approach P=1/(1+10^((pB-pA)/k)) is the right level of complexity. Don't over-engineer the win probability function.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Multi-source blending outperforms any single model
key_takeaways.action: Our 3-layer blend (40% stats + 45% market + 15% factors) mirrors FiveThirtyEight's multi-source approach. The 45% market weight is well-supported.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Simple seed-based predictions are surprisingly hard to beat
key_takeaways.action: Our model must significantly outperform seed-based baseline. Track seed-only bracket as benchmark.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Travel distance matters for neutral-site games
key_takeaways.action: Consider adding travel distance as a factor in our qualitative adjustments (P_factors). FiveThirtyEight found it significant.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Close games are essentially coin flips regardless of model
key_takeaways.action: Don't over-invest in predicting close games. Focus model calibration on identifying games that WON'T be close.
Our simulation engine should support pool-optimized bracket selection (contrarian picks based on ownership data). This is separate from game prediction accuracy.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Genetic algorithms reveal upset team profiles
key_takeaways.action: Guard-heavy teams with high steal rates and 3PT attempt rates are upset candidates. Build this into our upset detection.

key_takeaways.takeaway: 12-over-5 upset is the most reliable prediction
key_takeaways.action: Ensure our simulation produces at least one 12-over-5 upset in ~90% of brackets. Historical rate is 35.6% per matchup.
Our CLAUDE.md already excludes 'recent form (last 10 games)' from power index. This is validated by research.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Stratified importance sampling IS the state-of-art for bracket generation
key_takeaways.action: Our approach of generating diversified bracket portfolios covering multiple scenarios aligns with what the best bracket optimization services do (TeamRankings/PoolGenius).

key_takeaways.takeaway: The 8-vs-9 matchup is a coin flip (9-seed actually wins MORE)
key_takeaways.action: Our simulation should reflect this. Historical: 9-seeds win 51.9% against 8-seeds.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Dynamic rating updates between tournament rounds improve accuracy
key_takeaways.action: Consider implementing Bayesian weight updates after each round in our tracker (we already plan this via weight normalization).

key_takeaways.takeaway: Brier score of 0.1589 is achievable with LSTM + Brier loss
key_takeaways.action: Our target of 0.205 is conservative. With our blend approach, aim for 0.18-0.20 range.
